items.prompt_md: In the stack + shadow-stack design, what does `_mins[i]` represent?
options: The minimum of the entire push history, including values that have since been popped
The minimum of `_data[:i+1]` — everything pushed up to and including index i
The index of the smallest value currently in `_data`
The difference between `_data[i]` and the previous minimum
items.answer: The minimum of `_data[:i+1]` — everything pushed up to and including index i
items.explanation_md: Each slot in `_mins` snapshots the minimum of the data stack through that same push, so popping both lists in lockstep always leaves `_mins[-1]` correct for whatever remains.

items.prompt_md: You need a stack with push, pop, and a `get_min()` that must run in O(1), not by rescanning. Which design fits?
options: Keep a second stack that stores, at each push, the minimum of everything currently on the main stack
Sort the stack's contents on every push
Store values in a dict keyed by value and scan its keys for the smallest
Recompute the minimum with a full scan every time get_min is called
items.answer: Keep a second stack that stores, at each push, the minimum of everything currently on the main stack
items.explanation_md: Push/pop plus an O(1) query over only the current contents is the fingerprint for a shadow structure updated in lockstep with the primary one — the same composition move used elsewhere in this unit.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the stack + shadow-stack min-stack design, across n operations?
options: O(1) time per operation, O(n) extra space
O(n) time per operation, O(1) extra space
O(log n) time per operation, O(n) extra space
O(1) time per operation, O(1) extra space
items.answer: O(1) time per operation, O(n) extra space
items.explanation_md: Every operation only touches the top of one or two lists — O(1) time — but the shadow stack duplicates one entry per push, so extra space is O(n).
